question: I'm a salary band I staff member who was directly involved in supervising a credit institution. If I want to leave the ECB to join a direct competitor of that bank, how long is my cooling-off period, and do I also have a notification obligation toward the ECB?
answer: A cooling-off period of six months applies before starting work for a direct competitor of a credit institution in whose supervision the staff member at salary band I or above was directly involved, running from the date direct involvement ceased. Separately, staff at salary band I or above involved in supervisory activities must notify the Compliance and Governance Office before accepting any occupational activity for a period of two years from the date their involvement in supervisory activities ceased. (ECB Staff Rules, pages 9–10)

question: If a colleague offers me a small gift worth around EUR 30 at a work-related lunch, can I keep it without registering it, given the general prohibition on accepting advantages?
answer: Members of staff may neither solicit nor accept any advantage connected with their employment at the ECB. As an exception, hospitality offered by the private sector with a value of up to EUR 50 may be accepted in the context of a work-related occasion, provided it is not frequent and not from the same source, and provided it is not offered by current or potential suppliers or by credit institutions in the context of on-site inspections or audits. Such hospitality within these conditions does not need to be registered. (ECB Staff Rules, pages 5–6)

question: I want to do a transaction in euro-area government bonds worth EUR 15,000 in a single month. Do I need authorisation, and from whom, and how long before the order should I submit the request?
answer: Transactions exceeding EUR 10,000 within any given calendar month in government securities issued by euro area Member States are subject to prior authorisation by the Compliance and Governance Office. Any such request must be submitted at least five working days prior to the envisaged order date, and the Office shall decide on the request within five working days; if it does not react within five working days, the transaction is deemed authorised. (ECB Staff Rules, pages 15–17)

question: I joined the ECB four years ago and was placed under a convertible contract. What is the minimum performance threshold to convert my contract, and who decides on the conversion if I'm employed below the I band?
answer: Convertible contracts may be converted into an indefinite contract subject to the interest of the service and at least overall satisfactory performance of the member of staff over the period of the convertible contract. For members of staff assigned to a post below the I band, the conversion decision is taken by the Director General Human Resources or their Deputy, after consulting the Director General or Director of the business area in which the member of staff is employed, at least six months prior to the expiry of the convertible contract. (ECB Staff Rules, pages 44–45)

question: If I want to apply internally for another position at the ECB, am I eligible if I haven't yet completed my probationary period, and how long does my probationary period normally last?
answer: Eligibility for internal selection procedures is reserved for members of staff with an indefinite or fixed-term contract, and graduate programme participants, who have successfully completed their probationary period at the closing date for applications. Appointments are subject to a probationary period of six months unless the Chief Services Officer, on behalf of the Executive Board, decides to waive it, although in exceptional circumstances a longer period may be determined. (ECB Staff Rules, pages 33, 47)

question: I'm a part-time staff member and I'd like to know whether my child allowance is also pro-rated like my basic salary, and whether my annual leave is.
answer: For part-time staff, the basic salary is prorated to the time worked, and the expatriation and household allowances and their minimum amounts are calculated on the basis of the prorated salary; however, the staff member shall continue to receive the full child allowance and the full education allowance. Annual leave entitlement is calculated on a pro-rata basis and rounded up to the nearest half hour. (ECB Staff Rules, pages 88–89)

question: I'd like to telework from a country outside the EU for a couple of weeks. What is the annual cap on this, and within that period, what is the maximum I can do consecutively?
answer: Out of the maximum 90 working days per calendar year of teleworking from a location within the Union, members of staff may telework from a location outside the Union for up to 20 working days per calendar year. In addition, teleworking shall not exceed a maximum of 10 consecutive working days, a limit that applies both within and across calendar months and also where teleworking is combined with leave, since leave does not interrupt the consecutiveness. (ECB Staff Rules, pages 83–84)

question: I'm finalising my onboarding. By when must I claim my benefits on appointment, and within what timeframe should I have submitted the request to relocate?
answer: Members of staff shall claim their benefits on appointment within one year after completion of their probationary period. To be entitled to benefits on appointment, the staff member's request to relocate must be approved by the Directorate General Human Resources prior to their date of appointment, in addition to the place of appointment being Frankfurt am Main and the place of recruitment being more than 50 kilometres from the ECB. (ECB Staff Rules, pages 70, 72)

question: Two of my colleagues participated in a selection committee for a position one of them later wanted to apply for. What rules govern the salary band of selection committee members, and could a candidate be disqualified for having privately contacted one of them?
answer: Members of staff in the selection committee shall be at a salary band that is at least one salary band higher than the position(s) to be filled, with limited exceptions for the Directorate General Human Resources member and at most one other member enhancing diversity at the same salary band. Separately, the selection committee may decide to disqualify candidates from the selection procedure if they contact or attempt to contact a member of the selection committee in an undue manner. (ECB Staff Rules, pages 36, 39)

question: I think I'm being targeted with inappropriate behaviour by a colleague. What informal options do I have before formally reporting, and how long do I have to request protection if I later face retaliation for reporting?
answer: Members of staff who consider themselves the target of inappropriate behaviour, or who have witnessed it, may seek informal support from a trusted person of their choice (including a staff representative or a counterpart in DG Human Resources), a Social Counsellor, or the Mediator, and shall not suffer any negative consequences for doing so unless they are later found to have made deliberately false or malicious statements. If retaliation occurs, whistleblowers and witnesses may submit a request for protection from retaliation to the Chief Compliance and Governance Officer within 24 months from the time of the occurrence of the alleged retaliation. (ECB Staff Rules, pages 22, 27)

question: I'm participating in a strike. Will my salary be deducted, and what is the minimum lead time the organising body must give to the Executive Board?
answer: Unless the Executive Board decides otherwise, the total period of the strike shall be deducted from the salary and salary-related payments of the member of staff taking part. The organising body shall inform the Executive Board in writing of the intention to strike at least ten working days before the first day of the strike, stating the precise nature of the dispute, the precise nature of the proposed strike action, and the period during which the strike is going to take place. (ECB Staff Rules, page 31)

question: Following the second consecutive annual review showing underperformance, what may happen, and who acts as the second reviewer in the annual review process?
answer: Where a second consecutive annual review establishes underperformance, an underperformance procedure may be initiated. The second reviewer is the first reviewer's direct line manager, although upon a proposal by the head of the relevant business area, the Director General Human Resources or their Deputy (or the Chief Services Officer for staff working in DG Human Resources) may decide that another staff member shall be the second reviewer or, exceptionally, that there shall be no second reviewer. (ECB Staff Rules, pages 48, 50)

question: I'm being asked to take a non-confirmation decision on a manager I appointed. How long is the confirmation period for the manager, and what happens to them as a result of a non-confirmation decision?
answer: The confirmation period for an appointment to a managerial position at a higher salary band, or for a first appointment in a managerial position at the same salary band, is six months, and may be extended by the appointing authority if the member of staff is prevented by illness, accident, maternity or, exceptionally, special leave from performing their duties for more than one month. As a result of a decision of non-confirmation, the member of staff shall be reassigned to a position corresponding to their level prior to the non-confirmed appointment, and where the non-confirmation relates to a first appointment in a managerial position at the same salary band, the reassignment shall be to a non-managerial position at that level. (ECB Staff Rules, page 43)

question: If a colleague at salary band G has a child with a learning disability, can the cost of additional specialised educational support fall under "imperative educational needs", and what is the role of the ECB Medical Adviser in confirming this?
answer: A child with a learning disability or a neuro-behavioural developmental disorder, requiring additional specialised educational support suitable for their condition while attending a regular school, is among the situations satisfying the condition of imperative educational needs for the education allowance, where this is certified by the ECB Medical Adviser. The Medical Adviser is entitled to examine and periodically re-examine the child, refer the child for a specialist opinion if required, and ask the child's doctor for relevant information with the staff member's consent. (ECB Staff Rules, pages 64–65, 68)

question: I want to start an external occupational activity. Do I need authorisation, and once authorised, may I carry it out during working hours?
answer: Members of staff shall obtain written authorisation before engaging in an external activity that is of an occupational nature or goes otherwise beyond what can be reasonably considered a leisure activity; the Director General Human Resources or their Deputy, after consulting the Compliance and Governance Office and the relevant line managers, grants such authorisation if the activity does not impair the staff member's professional duties or constitute a likely source of conflict of interest, for a maximum of five years at a time. Members of staff shall perform external activities outside working hours, although on an exceptional basis the Director General Human Resources or their Deputy may authorise derogations from this rule. (ECB Staff Rules, pages 7–8)
